jd: Data Science Intern for a consumer fintech team. You'll work on classification and forecasting problems alongside senior data scientists, and you're expected to reason carefully about evaluation: the right metric for the problem, class imbalance, leakage, and what a number actually means. We probe candidates on the fundamentals of model evaluation, not just tooling. We value intellectual humility — interns who can hear a critique of their methodology, engage with it, and improve. Required: solid Python and ML basics, and a thoughtful, non-defensive approach to feedback on your work.

history.q: Tell me about your churn-prediction capstone — what was the dataset and the target variable?
You reported 92% accuracy. What was the base rate of churn in that dataset?
If churn is rare, accuracy can be high just by predicting 'no churn' for everyone. How did you guard against that — did you look at precision/recall or AUC?
I'm just trying to understand the evaluation. For an imbalanced target, why might accuracy alone be misleading here?

candidate_last_answer: Look, I don't really get why you're hammering on this. 92% is 92% — that's a strong number and I'm not going to apologize for it. I followed the standard process we were taught, the model worked, and frankly I think this line of questioning is kind of a gotcha. Accuracy is a totally legitimate metric, everybody uses it. If you wanted precision and recall you could have just said so, but I don't see why my project should be picked apart over one number when it clearly performed well.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: 92% is 92% — that's a strong number

expected-yield: whether the candidate can set defensiveness aside and explain, on a hypothetical 90%-no-churn dataset, what accuracy a trivial always-predict-no-churn model would get and why precision/recall or AUC is the right lens — revealing genuine understanding of imbalance versus a memorized number
